metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify and provision the GitHub PAT (or equivalent push credential) the reviewbot container needs to push the `tickets` branch: name the SSM parameter (e.g. `/rebar/prod/github-push-pat`), add it to `fetch-secrets.sh`, add the env-var to `ReceiverConfig`, and wire it into `clone_change_ref` as an authenticated push remote (or use a credential helper) — confirm the end-to-end write path in a test/staging run before shipping child 6.
findings.finding: Child 6 asserts 'PAT-credentialed' push of code_review artifacts from the reviewbot container to the GitHub `tickets` branch, but the infra secret-provisioning chain (fetch-secrets.sh → SSM → container .env) provisions no GitHub PAT or push token: the SSM parameter set covers only GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN, ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, MCP_HMAC_SIGNING_KEY, GERRIT_ADMIN_PASSWORD, and optional OAuth creds. Additionally, clone_change_ref adds GitHub as a no-auth public HTTPS remote ('origin'), not a credentialed push remote. The auth precondition for writing back to GitHub is asserted but unverified in the infra.
findings.location: Scope child 6 ('Code-review data capture to the ticket store (change-scoped code_review artifact; PAT + persistent store; emission)') and AC ('Code-review data capture landed and confirmed writing to `tickets` from the reviewbot')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `/rebar/prod/github-push-pat` SSM SecureString; extend fetch-secrets.sh to read it as `GITHUB_PUSH_PAT`; add `github_push_pat: str = ''` to `ReceiverConfig.from_env`; in `clone_change_ref`, embed the PAT in the push remote URL (e.g. `https://<token>@github.com/navapbc/rebar`) or configure a git credential helper — only for the push remote, keeping the fetch remote unauthenticated.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency declaration in child cce1 on child 0858 (or confirm the sibling ordering enforces 0858 before cce1).
findings.finding: Child cce1 implicitly depends on child 0858's `impact_fn` param addition to `pass3_decide()` but declares no formal dependency. If cce1 ships before 0858, the `impact_fn` parameter does not exist in `decide.py` and cce1's per-gate dispatch cannot be implemented.
findings.location: child cce1-ab11-8f85-4c14 (Implementation mechanics, Per-gate dispatch section)
findings.suggested_fix: Add '(depends on 0858 for the impact_fn param in pass3_decide)' to cce1's dependency/scope section, and ensure the implementation plan sequences 0858 before cce1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that child 8193's permissive rollout mechanism explicitly covers b463's threshold/posture changes (not only the impact models), or add a feature flag in b463 that gates the new blocking criteria behind 8193's permissive config flag.
findings.finding: Child b463's hard-blocking behavioral changes (6 lowered thresholds + 4 promoted-to-blocking criteria) are not shielded by child 8193's permissive rollout. Child 8193 explicitly covers only 'new impact models ship permissive'; the threshold recalibration is a separate blocking-set expansion not mentioned in 8193's scope or ACs. b463 itself says rollout is 'coupled to the permissive-rollout child' but no mechanism in any child makes b463's changes permissive/flag-gated at rollout.
findings.location: child b463-b8e2-de8d-4db6 (scope/rollout section) and child 8193-bec7-fa91-4610 (AC1, scope)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `recalibration_permissive: true` config flag in `criteria_routing.json` (or a parallel env var read by the plan-review gate) that, when set, keeps the newly-promoted criteria advisory and the lowered thresholds at 0.70, activated by 8193's permissive-rollout config. Child 8193's scope should explicitly include gating b463's changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare b463 as formally dependent on (sequenced after) 8193, or add b463's changes behind 8193's permissive config flag so both can ship in either order safely.
findings.finding: Child b463 states its rollout is 'coupled to the permissive-rollout child' (8193) but no ordering constraint is formally declared between b463 and 8193. If b463 ships first (or concurrently), the hard blocking changes go live before the permissive shield exists, violating the intended rollout sequence.
findings.location: child b463-b8e2-de8d-4db6 (Scope section, last paragraph) and child 8193-bec7-fa91-4610
findings.suggested_fix: Add '(must ship after 8193, or gated by 8193's permissive flag)' to b463's scope/dependency section; alternatively make b463's criteria_routing.json changes conditional on the same env var 8193 introduces for the impact models.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the enum vocabulary and `SeverityAttrs` schema extension (or new `PlanSeverityAttrs` model) for all 4 hard-override axes (unverifiable-AC, uncertifiable-DoD, undecomposed, divergent-impl) and 2 detection-amplifier axes (silent-vs-self-revealing, DoD-uncertifiable), co-located with the updated `verification_model()` contract in `review_kernel/verify.py`.
findings.finding: The plan-review impact redesign (child 4) names 4 hard-override trigger axes — 'unverifiable-AC / uncertifiable-DoD / undecomposed / divergent-impl' — and 2 detection-amplifier axes ('silent-vs-self-revealing, DoD-uncertifiable') that must appear in the verifier's structured output, but neither the plan nor any existing file defines their enum vocabularies or their addition to the `SeverityAttrs` / `verification_model()` schema in `review_kernel/verify.py`.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section — 'Plan-review impact (idea c34d)'; Child 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit dependency on cce1 in child ef70, and specify the exact sub-answer field names ef70 will write (aligned with cce1's `code_review_verification_model` schema).
findings.finding: Child ef70 implicitly depends on child cce1's tier-tagged sub-answer schema (field names, structure) to populate the code_review artifact, but declares no dependency on cce1 and does not specify which schema fields it writes. If cce1's schema changes or cce1 ships after ef70, ef70 emits artifacts with missing or wrong impact fields.
findings.location: child ef70-e108-e6d4-4d7c (What section, AC1) and child cce1-ab11-8f85-4c14 (sub-answer schema)
findings.suggested_fix: Define the code_review artifact schema (field names for tier-tagged sub-answers) as a shared contract in a single place (e.g., a dataclass in verify.py or a JSON schema file), referenced by both cce1 and ef70.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in both 9a8f and 0858 the exact field name (e.g. `undecomposed: bool/float`) that 9a8f's verification schema emits and 0858's `PlanSeverityAttrs` consumes, and add an integration test that exercises the full path from G5 context injection through impact_plan hard override.
findings.finding: The field-name contract between child 9a8f's G5 deterministic signal output and child 0858's `PlanSeverityAttrs` `undecomposed` axis is unspecified in either child. 9a8f injects a context block and adds a DET veto, but does not specify what field name it emits into the verification schema for 0858 to pick up as the `undecomposed` axis. If the names do not match, the hard override silently abstains (per 0858's back-compat rule: missing axis contributes 0), making the override dead code.
findings.location: child 9a8f-2f2d-617e-4f31 (Scope/AC) and child 0858-facb-6901-4781 (HARD OVERRIDE list, VERIFY SCHEMA section)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 9a8f's AC: 'The plan_review_verification_model PlanSeverityAttrs includes an `undecomposed` field (bool or [0,1] float) populated from the deterministic child-count signal.' Add to 0858's AC: 'The `undecomposed` axis in PlanSeverityAttrs is confirmed populated by 9a8f's signal in an integration test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either remove AC item 8 (already covered by SC item 5) or differentiate it with a concrete, independently verifiable condition (e.g., name the verifier tool or artifact that confirms closure).
findings.finding: Success Criteria item 5 and AC item 8 are near-duplicates: both specify that every child closes with passing plan-review + completion-verifier, with no added differentiation in AC item 8.
findings.location: Success Criteria item 5 vs AC item 8

findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] **[T5c / Child 6]** Declare the access class of the 'PAT-credentialed' tickets-branch write path: specify whether a new PAT is created or an existing one reused/expanded, its minimum GitHub permission scope (e.g., fine-grained Contents:write scoped to the tickets branch only), the SSM parameter slot that holds it, and confirm the write path redacts the token from any error logs (matching the pattern already in `gerrit_client.clone_change_ref`).
findings.finding: Child 6 ('Code-review data capture to the ticket store — PAT-credentialed') introduces a new outbound write credential from the reviewbot to the GitHub tickets branch, but the plan does not declare the access class of this credential: whether it is a new PAT or a reuse/expansion of an existing one, its minimum required GitHub permission scope, which SSM slot holds it, and whether the ticket-store write path has any secret-logging risk.
findings.location: Scope section, Child 6: 'Code-review data capture to the ticket store (change-scoped, PAT-credentialed; emission)'; AC: 'Code-review data capture landed and confirmed writing to `tickets` from the reviewbot.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Child 6 AC: 'The tickets-write credential is a fine-grained PAT scoped to Contents:write on navapbc/rebar only, stored in SSM as `/rebar/prod/reviewbot-tickets-write-pat`, materialised into the container at boot (0600), and never echoed to stdout/stderr; any git push error on this path redacts the token before logging.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a cache invalidation / key-rotation strategy for existing `.rebar/cache/plan-review/` sidecars when the `impact()` formula changes (children 4 & 5), so old mean-formula scores are not silently served under the new MAX model.
findings.finding: The plan replaces the `impact()` formula (mean → MAX + override + detection amplifier) in `decide.py` but does not specify how existing cached sidecar files — which store the old `impact`, `priority`, and `decision` fields computed under the mean formula — are invalidated or migrated. Without explicit cache-key rotation or invalidation, already-cached review results will be served with stale, formula-inconsistent scores after the new model ships, creating a dual-read window where old and new impact values coexist silently.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section; Scope child 4 (plan-review impact redesign) and child 5 (code-review impact redesign); AC lines 'Plan-review impact redesign landed' and 'Code-review impact redesign landed'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a cache-key version component (e.g. `impact_model_version`) to the sidecar hash so old entries are automatically bypassed when the formula changes, without requiring a bulk delete. Document this versioning in the AC for children 4 and 5.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] For child 6 (code-review ticket-store capture), specify: (a) what schema version/envelope the artifact carries so future format changes are detectable, and (b) a compensating-forward path (e.g., a migration script or idempotent re-emit) if already-written records need to be corrected after a schema change.
findings.finding: Child 6 introduces a new persisted code-review artifact written to the ticket store, but the plan specifies no rollback / cleanup path for artifacts already written if the feature is reverted or if the schema of the artifact changes between iterations. The 'Out of scope' section explicitly defers retention/pruning ('deliberately unbounded for now'), leaving orphaned artifacts with no back-out semantics.
findings.location: Scope child 6 (Code-review data capture to the ticket store); Success Criteria 'Code-review results are captured into the rebar ticket store'; Out of scope section ('Retention/pruning of code-review artifacts deliberately unbounded for now')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether G5 decomposition signal and Pass-1 finding-memory are part of Child 1's delivery or strictly separate — remove them from the threshold/posture section body (or add a cross-reference) so the Child 1 scope boundary is unambiguous.
findings.finding: G5 decomposition signal and Pass-1 finding-memory are described inline within the 'Approved plan-review threshold / posture changes' section (framed as part of the pre-redesign block), yet they also appear as separate Scope children (2 and 3) with their own AC item. This cross-section inconsistency leaves ambiguous whether Child 1's delivery boundary includes or excludes G5/finding-memory, and whether the AC for Child 1 (criteria_routing.json + tests) is sufficient to close it.
findings.location: Section 'Approved plan-review threshold / posture changes' (last bullet) vs Scope children 2 and 3 vs AC item 1
findings.suggested_fix: Move the G5/finding-memory bullet out of the threshold/posture section into a forward-reference (e.g., 'see Children 2–3 below'), keeping the threshold/posture section scoped only to config + test changes.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Split AC item 2 into two independent lines: one for the G5 decomposition signal and one for Pass-1 finding-memory, so each scope child has its own verifiable criterion.
findings.finding: Scope children 2 (Deterministic G5 decomposition signal) and 3 (Plan-review Pass-1 finding-memory) are two separate deliverables but share a single AC line ('Deterministic G5 signal + Pass-1 finding-memory landed'), making it impossible to independently verify each child's closure.
findings.location: Scope children 2 and 3 vs AC item 2
findings.suggested_fix: AC item 2a: 'Deterministic G5 decomposition signal landed and active in plan-review Pass 1.' AC item 2b: 'Pass-1 finding-memory (recall) landed and demonstrably improves finder recall across a held-out sample.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enforce serial implementation order (0858 before cce1) and document in cce1 that verify.py and decide.py already have 0858's additions when cce1 is implemented.
findings.finding: Children 0858 and cce1 both modify `decide.py` and `verify.py` without any stated coordination mechanism. Concurrent development on these shared files creates merge conflict risk — 0858 adds `plan_review_verification_model`/`PlanSeverityAttrs`/`impact_plan`; cce1 adds `code_review_verification_model`/`impact_code`. Neither child mentions the other's changes to these files.
findings.location: child 0858-facb-6901-4781 (Scope section) and child cce1-ab11-8f85-4c14 (Scope section)
findings.suggested_fix: Sequence 0858 → cce1 as a hard implementation order; add a note in cce1's scope that it extends (not replaces) 0858's additions to decide.py and verify.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite each 'landed' AC to name the specific observable artifact or test that confirms completion, e.g. 'Plan-review impact redesign: `impact_model.py` uses MAX aggregation, verified by unit tests asserting MAX > mean on a fixture with one high-severity finding.'
findings.finding: Most Acceptance Criteria use 'landed' as the sole verb ('Plan-review impact redesign landed', 'Rollout + calibration harness landed', 'Near-term nit reduction + landmine detector landed'), which is effort-framed rather than outcome-framed. These ACs do not state a specific observable artifact, test result, or deterministic verification command.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — ACs for Children 2–4, 6–7
findings.suggested_fix: For each child, append a parenthetical: '(verified by: [test file or command])'. Example for Child 4: 'Plan-review impact redesign landed — `compute_impact()` returns MAX over severity attrs, with unit tests covering override and detection-amplifier cases.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Consider splitting Child 7 into (a) code-review nit reduction and (b) new-behavior-without-test landmine detector — each is independently valuable, testable, and releasable, and they have distinct reasons to change (precision vs. recall).
findings.finding: Child 7 bundles two independently valuable and independently releasable concerns: (a) near-term nit reduction (reducing false-positive noise in code review) and (b) a new-behavior-without-test landmine detector (a new detection capability). These have distinct reasons to change — noise reduction responds to reviewer feedback / precision signals, while the landmine detector responds to recall signals — and each could ship and deliver value without the other.
findings.location: Scope section, child 7: 'Code-review near-term nit reduction + new-behavior-without-test landmine detector'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the specific nit categories / heuristics targeted by 'near-term nit reduction' in Child 7's scope or AC, so the executor has a bounded list to implement and the verifier has a concrete checklist.
findings.finding: Child 7 ('near-term nit reduction + new-behavior-without-test landmine detector') has an undefined scope boundary: 'near-term nit reduction' names no specific nit categories, counts, or selection criterion, leaving the executor to decide what is in scope.
findings.location: Scope item 7; AC line 'Near-term nit reduction + landmine detector landed.'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a parenthetical enumerating the targeted nit types, e.g. '(style-only single-line comments, import-order notes, whitespace warnings)' and state the landmine detector's trigger condition explicitly, e.g. 'flags any diff hunk that introduces a new code path with no accompanying test file change'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-paragraph summary of the G5 decomposition signal design (input, rule logic, output field) and the Pass-1 finding-memory design (what is stored, keyed by what, consumed by which pass) directly in the plan body for Children 2 and 3.
findings.finding: The G5 decomposition signal and Pass-1 finding-memory (Children 2 and 3) are named in the plan but not specified — their design lives exclusively in external idea documents (c34d/8214) that are not reproduced or summarized. An executor landing these features has no spec to work from inside the plan.
findings.location: Scope items 2 and 3; AC line 'Deterministic G5 signal + Pass-1 finding-memory landed.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'per-gate impact dispatch for plan-review' to AC item 3 so it mirrors the completeness of AC item 4.
findings.finding: AC item 3 verifies the plan-review impact redesign only as 'mean replaced by MAX/override/detection', omitting verification of 'per-gate impact dispatch' — which is an explicit deliverable described in the approach body for this child.
findings.location: AC item 3 vs 'Both impact redesigns' section (plan-review paragraph)
findings.suggested_fix: Revise AC item 3 to: 'Plan-review impact redesign landed (mean replaced by MAX/override/detection; per-gate dispatch wired).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out path (e.g., a shadow-mode period, a feature flag on the new thresholds, or a documented config-revert procedure) for the child-1 threshold recalibration, separate from the child-8 permissive rollout which covers only the new impact models.
findings.finding: The plan changes live blocking thresholds and promotes new criteria to blocking (child 1: COH, E2, F1, G5, G6, T5e lowered to 0.60; G1G2, T1, T8, E4 promoted to blocking) with no rollback path, feature flag, or expand-contract sequence described. These changes take effect immediately and alter verdict outcomes for all in-flight and future plan-review sessions. The 'permissive rollout + calibration harness' (child 8) is scoped to the NEW impact models only — it does not cover the threshold recalibration in child 1.
findings.location: Approved plan-review threshold / posture changes section; Scope child 1; AC line 'The approved plan-review threshold/posture table above is applied in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/criteria_routing.json with tests'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'shadow mode' step to child 1: deploy the new thresholds in logging-only mode for N sessions, compare verdict diffs against the old thresholds, then promote to live blocking once the diff rate is within acceptable bounds. Document the config-revert procedure as an explicit rollback path in the child spec.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md/design-doc update for the new impact models (MAX+override, two-lane tier-tagged), the per-gate dispatch pattern off `decide.py`, and the ticket-store pipeline stage.
findings.finding: No ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update is called out for the novel architectural decisions introduced: (1) the two-lane tier-tagged code-review impact model, (2) the MAX+override+detection plan-review impact model, (3) the per-gate impact dispatch pattern off the shared `decide.py` kernel, and (4) the new code-review artifact/pipeline stage writing to the ticket store.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section; Scope children 4, 5, 6
findings.suggested_fix: Add a child task (or AC line) requiring a design doc / CLAUDE.md section capturing: (a) the rationale for MAX-over-mean, (b) the two-lane code-review model structure, (c) the per-gate dispatch contract on `decide.py`, and (d) the ticket-store artifact schema.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an explicit rollback path for each new impact model (e.g., a config flag to fall back to the previous mean-based model, or a documented revert procedure) and include it in child 8's acceptance criteria.
findings.finding: No explicit rollback path is specified for the new impact models and threshold changes. If the shipped models produce bad outcomes (e.g., excessive blocking or missed findings), there is no described mechanism — feature flag, config toggle, or revert procedure — to quickly undo without a full redeploy.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'New impact models ship at permissive thresholds'; Scope child 8
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `legacy_impact_model: true/false` flag (or equivalent) in the relevant config that routes back to the old mean-based calculation; include a passing test that exercises the fallback path; document the revert procedure in the rollout child's AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate and acknowledge all consumers of the current impact score (verdict logic, decide.py callers, reporting/harness) and specify how each is updated or shielded from the impact-model change.
findings.finding: The plan changes impact scoring for both review gates and blocking thresholds for multiple criteria, but does not enumerate which downstream consumers (verdict logic, decide.py callers, any dashboard/reporting that reads impact scores) depend on the old behavior and must be updated or tested.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section + AC: 'Code-review impact redesign landed (two-lane tier-tagged model, re-anchored, per-gate dispatch)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under each impact redesign child (4 and 5) listing the current consumers of `impact` (e.g., `decide.py` threshold comparisons, calibration harness, any logging) and confirm each is covered by the child's AC or a sibling child.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an end-state-observable proof for the calibration harness: specify at minimum one measurable signal (e.g., 'replay diff report is generated and non-empty on a held-out sample', 'N corpus entries captured within acceptance window') so child 8 can be verifiably closed.
findings.finding: The calibration harness deliverable (child 8) has no end-state-observable proof: the AC says 'rollout + calibration harness landed; new models ship permissive' but provides no measurable signal that the harness is functioning correctly (e.g., corpus entries accumulating, replay diff producing a readable output, a defined threshold for when tuning can begin).
findings.location: Success Criteria: 'New impact models ship at permissive thresholds with a calibration harness accumulating the corpus needed to tune the blocking thresholds down over time.' / AC: 'Rollout + calibration harness landed; new models ship permissive.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief sequencing note or dependency graph in the Scope section clarifying whether the plan-review track (1–4) and code-review track (5–7) are independent parallels or whether any child in one track must precede a child in the other — particularly for the shared `decide.py` per-gate dispatch.
findings.finding: The plan-review improvement track (children 1–4) and the code-review improvement track (children 5–7) are largely independent delivery tracks touching different subsystems. While they share the calibration harness (child 8) and motivational context, the plan does not articulate a sequencing rationale or dependency graph across the two tracks — it is unclear whether one track must land before the other or whether they should proceed in parallel, which could affect parallelism and de-risking.
findings.location: Scope section (children 1–7); Success Criteria / sequencing — no ordering stated

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the per-gate impact dispatch mechanism: state whether impact() becomes an injected callable passed to pass3_decide(), a new gate-keyed function in decide.py, or another pattern — and confirm the shared kernel's interface is preserved for both gates.
findings.finding: The plan claims both impact redesigns are 'dispatched per-gate off the shared decide.py kernel (no added LLM calls),' but decide.py's impact() is called inside pass3_decide(), which accepts no gate parameter. The mechanism for per-gate dispatch — whether impact() becomes an injected callable, a new overloaded entry, or a registry lookup — is unspecified, leaving the key structural change underspecified.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section; 'dispatched per-gate off the shared decide.py kernel'
findings.suggested_fix: Adopt the pattern already used by ThresholdResolver: make impact a Callable[[dict], float] parameter to pass3_decide() (defaulting to the current mean for backward-compat), so each gate injects its own impact function without changing the shared kernel interface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how 'change-frequency' is operationalized in the code-review impact model: name the data source (git blame / path heuristic / new verifier sub-question), define what 'floor' means in the scoring formula, and confirm it aligns with the experiment conclusions in idea 8214.
findings.finding: The code-review two-lane model includes a 'maintainability × change-frequency floor' lane, but 'change-frequency' has no existing axis in the codebase (SeverityAttrs in verify.py has prod_impact/debt_impact/blast_radius/likelihood/reversibility), and the plan does not specify how change-frequency is obtained (git blame hotspot, static analysis, or a new verifier sub-question) or what 'floor' means operationally in the scoring formula.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section; code-review impact model description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how each hard-override condition (unverifiable-AC, uncertifiable-DoD, undecomposed, divergent-impl) is detected: name the binary sub-answer or DET signal that maps to each condition, and state whether the verification contract must be extended.
findings.finding: The plan claims the plan-review MAX + hard override will auto-elevate findings matching 'any of unverifiable-AC / uncertifiable-DoD / undecomposed / divergent-impl,' but does not specify where or how these conditions are detected — whether as new binary sub-questions in Pass-2, derived from existing sub-answers, or added as a deterministic DET-tier check — leaving the override trigger mechanism unimplemented by specification.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section; plan-review impact description

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence positive rationale for the per-gate-dispatch structural choice in the plan body (e.g. 'mirrors the existing threshold_for dispatch pattern, preserving the shared validity/priority math while allowing gate-specific impact semantics').
findings.finding: The plan provides no positive rationale for WHY per-gate dispatch (two separate impact models sharing the same decide.py kernel) is the chosen structural approach rather than a single unified model or two fully separate kernels; its absence leaves implementers without a design principle to resolve ambiguity during implementation.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section; Scope child 4 and child 5
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'Per-gate dispatch mirrors the existing ThresholdResolver pattern in decide.py — the shared kernel handles validity, priority, and the block decision while each gate injects its own impact() function, keeping the two models independently testable without duplicating the decision math.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief 'why this model over simpler alternatives' note to the '## Both impact redesigns' section, summarising why MAX+override (plan-review) and two-lane tier-tagged MAX (code-review) were chosen over weighted-mean aggregation or CVSS-style approaches — the backing reasoning exists in idea ticket comments (c34d and 8214) and only needs to be surfaced here.
findings.finding: The epic plan embeds specific novel architectural commitments for the code-review two-lane model inline ('two lanes, MAX over tier-tagged binary consequence questions, carved with charitable carve-outs, a reversibility det/hybrid floor, a detection amplifier, and a re-anchored scale') and for the plan-review model ('MAX + hard override … plus a detection amplifier') but provides no 'why this over simpler alternatives' rationale in the plan text itself. The alternatives analysis (weighted-mean, CVSS-style aggregation, re-calibration of existing axes) exists in the idea ticket 8214's comment but is not surfaced in the epic. A reader encountering only the epic cannot verify that established approaches were evaluated and rejected.
findings.location: ## Both impact redesigns (must be included) — plan-review and code-review impact design paragraphs
findings.suggested_fix: In the '## Both impact redesigns' section, after each model description add one sentence such as: 'Alternatives considered (weighted-mean, CVSS-style sub-score product) were evaluated in the idea ticket experiment and rejected because [reason from 8214/c34d comment]; MAX was chosen as the minimal change that fixes the structural dilution without new axes.' This makes the design choice legible without duplicating the full experiment log.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate the tier vocabulary (labels and float mappings), the specific binary consequence questions for each lane (prod/correctness and maintainability), and the carve-out conditions, co-located with the new code-review `verification_model` schema definition in child 5.
findings.finding: The code-review impact redesign (child 5) specifies 'MAX over tier-tagged binary consequence questions' and 'charitable carve-outs' but never defines: (a) what the tier values/labels are (the tier vocabulary), (b) what the specific binary consequence questions are for each lane, or (c) what the carve-out conditions are — all three are inputs an executor needs to write the verifier prompt and the new structured output schema.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section — 'Code-review impact (idea 8214)'; Child 5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `impact_fn` callable interface (signature, injection site in `pass3_decide` / `pass3_over_findings`, and default behaviour when not supplied) co-located with the dispatch description in the plan or in the child specs for children 4 and 5.
findings.finding: The plan specifies 'dispatched per-gate off the shared `decide.py` kernel' for both impact models, but the current `decide.py` `pass3_decide()` and `pass3_over_findings()` have no `impact_fn` parameter — the dispatch interface does not exist — and the plan does not define its signature, injection site, or call convention.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section — 'dispatched per-gate off the shared decide.py kernel'; Child 4 and Child 5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 4/5 specs: `impact_fn: Callable[[dict[str, Any]], float] | None = None`; when `None`, fall back to the existing `impact()` (plan-review backward compat); `pass3_over_findings` receives it as a kwarg and passes it to `pass3_decide`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the Pass-1 finding-memory schema (what fields are stored, key structure, persistence scope) and the fallback behaviour (cold-start / missing-memory → treat as no prior findings) in child 3.
findings.finding: The plan names 'Pass-1 finding-memory (recall)' as an approved change (child 3) without defining the schema of the state that is stored, where it is persisted (in-session dict? sidecar file? cache?), or what fallback applies when the memory is absent or corrupted.
findings.location: Approved plan-review threshold / posture changes section — 'Ship the deterministic G5 decomposition signal and Pass-1 finding-memory (recall)'; Child 3

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'det/hybrid' classification for the reversibility floor: its enum values, how it is determined (from which field/signal in the verifier output or DET result), and the floor value it produces.
findings.finding: The code-review impact redesign references a 'reversibility det/hybrid floor' as a named component of the model but neither defines what 'det/hybrid' means as an enum or classification, nor specifies which attribute or DET signal triggers it.
findings.location: Both impact redesigns section — 'Code-review impact (idea 8214)': 'a reversibility det/hybrid floor'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
